meta.summary: Common Ground is a structured design language for an office-facing desktop agent product. It treats the launcher as an intent router and the workspace as an execution and review surface, so the UI serves autonomous agents instead of stopping at tool discovery.
meta.source-of-truth: This XML is the canonical design source. Every token, surface, component rule, and behavior must be addressable by stable node id so design changes are reviewable in git.

implementation-targets.target: Renderer theme tokens and semantic CSS custom properties.
Tailwind theme aliases for layout, spacing, radius, and color.
React component variants and window-specific presets.
BrowserWindow and renderer shell presets for workspace and launcher.

intent.product-mission: Give office workers a desktop assistant that can capture intent quickly, execute meaningful work through agents, keep progress legible, and return outcomes without adding visual noise.

traits.item: Works across meetings, documents, chat, browser tabs, and local files.
Needs fast entry points with low context switching cost.
Judges trust through clarity of status, approval, and result quality.

needs.item: Fast invocation from anywhere on desktop.
Confidence that the agent is doing the right thing and not disappearing into black boxes.
Clear distinction between suggestion, execution, approval, and handoff.

intent.experience-promise: Calm enough for all-day use, decisive enough for operational work, and shared enough that the product language always serves both the user goal and the agent workflow.

non-goals.item: Do not mimic a developer console as the primary visual identity.
Do not turn the launcher into a crowded marketplace of tools and plugins.
Do not use decorative gradients, gratuitous glassmorphism, or novelty motion.

layer.owns: color, typography, spacing, radius, elevation, motion
reusable primitives, patterns, and component states

boundaries.dependency-direction: foundation -> semantics -> surfaces -> components -> feature implementations

failure-semantics.rule: If a required foundation token is missing, the theme build must fail loudly.
If a semantic token is missing, the UI must not silently remap it to an unrelated state.
If a surface contract is incomplete, implement the smallest valid surface instead of inventing a new pattern.

principles.principle: Every surface must help the user do work and help the product express agent capability. If a pattern only serves style, it does not belong here.
The launcher captures intent, routes it, and escalates into agents. It is not only a result list.
Status, progress, approval, failure, and ownership must be visible before ornament.
Respect window chrome, keyboard flow, pointer precision, and long-running multitasking behavior.
Use dense information layout where useful, but keep hierarchy obvious and breathing room consistent.

Establish hierarchy with spacing, alignment, and rhythm before introducing borders, fills, or containers. Use framed blocks only when a surface truly needs isolation, such as windows, approvals, and critical overlays.
Prefer named tokens and explicit contracts over one-off visual decisions, so the system can evolve by editing XML.

work-loop.step: Capture intent from anywhere on desktop.
Confirm target, context, and automation level.
Run agent work with visible progress and approvals.
Review artifacts, tool activity, and unresolved blockers.
Return a result, next action, or resumable work state.

ui-objects.object: The user expression of what should happen now.
A durable unit of work that can plan, act, wait, or hand off.
A generated or referenced output such as file, message, note, or result summary.
A stop point that requires explicit human confirmation.
A summarized state that lets the user or another agent continue without reloading context.

attention-levels.level: Background information that should stay readable but quiet.
Current user focus or the next recommended action.
Agent-owned work, state, and progress indicators.
The flow is blocked or failed and needs resolution.

agent-states.state: Waiting for intent or resumed context.
Agent is synthesizing or scoping the task.
Human approval is required before continuing.
Agent cannot continue because of missing context or a failure.
Work is complete and ready for review or handoff.

confidence-states.state: Result is plausible and should be previewed.
Result is exploratory and should not auto-execute.

zone.purpose: todos, subagents, files, approvals, context details

rules.item: Left rail is navigational, never a dumping ground for diagnostics.
Center studio owns the primary narrative of work.
Right inspector is contextual and should collapse complexity, not duplicate the center.

window.placement: Top-centered within the active display and keyboard reach.

content-order.item: Suggested destinations or agent entry points
Ranked results grouped by apps, recent work, agents, and actions
Execution hint row with keyboard affordances

rules.item: The first interaction is always about intent, not browsing categories.
Agent entry is a first-class route, visually equal to application launch.
Results should show type, confidence, and shortcut without requiring dense iconography.
Approvals must interrupt with strong contrast, concise consequence language, and explicit primary action.
Every approval includes requested action, affected target, and downstream impact.
Destructive approval actions never share the same emphasis color as routine continue actions.

surface.role: background updates and resumable work signals

rules.item: Use notifications for completion, handoff, or unblock events, not for every intermediate step.
Notifications must include a direct resume or inspect action.
Transient toasts are secondary to durable activity history inside the product.

component.purpose: Capture a user objective in natural language and keyboard-first flow.

rules.item: Placeholder copy should suggest outcomes, not feature names.
Do not add leading icon clutter unless it communicates state or mode.

component.purpose: Represent an application, agent action, recent thread, or structured command.
Represent a resumable work item with status and recency.

rules.item: Primary line is human-readable task title.
Secondary line shows last update or blocking state, not internal ids.
Status icon color follows the semantic agent state contract.

component.purpose: Make long-running state legible without expanding panel density.
Show files, summaries, generated outputs, or external references.

rules.item: Card header states what the artifact is and who produced it.
Primary action is preview or open; destructive actions are secondary.
Use mono typography only for code, paths, ids, and machine data.

component.purpose: Pause automation and request explicit human input.

rules.item: Default layout order is consequence, target, optional detail, action row.
Reject and approve actions must never have equal visual weight.

component.purpose: Organize todos, files, subagents, and contextual metadata.

rules.item: Sections are independent and collapsible when content grows.
Each section needs an empty state, loading state, and overflow strategy.

keyboard.rule: Every high-frequency flow must be completable without pointer use.
Launcher selection uses up, down, enter, tab, and escape as primary controls.
Focus rings are mandatory on actionable controls and use the shared focus stroke token.

motion-rules.rule: Animate page transitions, overlay entry, and result selection; avoid perpetual decorative motion.
Progress indicators may pulse only while state is changing.
Respect reduced motion and replace movement with opacity or color shifts.

density-rules.rule: Compact rows are acceptable in rails and lists.
Decision points and artifact reviews require more spacing than navigation surfaces.
Do not mix more than three density levels in one window.

window-chrome.rule: Respect draggable regions, platform traffic-light safe areas, and input controls as non-drag zones.
Launcher window feels like a precise overlay, not a full application modal.
Main workspace must preserve layout stability while panels resize.

platform-behavior.rule: Keyboard shortcuts, focus return, and top-level positioning are part of the design contract.
Do not rely on browser-only hover behavior for critical actions.
System notifications should mirror the product semantic model and deep-link back into work.

performance-budget.rule: Surface transitions should feel immediate at office-laptop performance levels.
Shadows, translucency, and animation cannot compromise input latency or typing comfort.

accessibility.rule: Body text contrast target is at least 4.5 to 1 in both day and focus themes.
Minimum interactive hit area is 32 by 32 pixels for dense desktop controls.
Color never acts as the only carrier of agent status or approval severity.
Empty, loading, error, and blocked states are required for every major surface and inspector section.

versioning.rule: Patch updates tune token values or wording without changing meaning.
Minor updates add tokens, components, or surfaces in backward-compatible ways.
Major updates rename or remove stable ids, semantic states, or required contracts.

editing-rules.rule: Add new design decisions as new nodes with stable ids instead of rewriting unrelated sections.
Keep one concern per node so git diffs stay reviewable.
When a token changes, update every referencing semantic or component contract in the same commit.

export-contract.renderer-strategy: Map foundation tokens to CSS custom properties, then alias semantic tokens per surface.
export-contract.component-strategy: Each component id maps to a variant key or recipe in the renderer UI layer.
export-contract.window-strategy: Surface ids map to Electron window presets and renderer shell layouts.
